key_insight: CuCrZr’s nano-precipitation hardening is not stable under fusion-relevant irradiation: precipitates dissolve or re-form depending on temperature, while transmutation and defect formation further degrade the alloy.
engineering_relevance: This challenges the long-term reliability of CuCrZr as a heat-sink material in fusion reactors and points to the need for new Cu-based alloys that retain strength under neutron exposure.
method: In situ transmission electron microscopy during heavy-ion irradiation and He implantation
Comparison of low-temperature ballistic dissolution versus high-temperature dissolution/re-precipitation
evidence_signal: The abstract reports direct microstructural observations showing precipitate degradation, plus modelling of helium bubbles, Kr-rich voids, and five-year transmutation toward Ni-Zr intermetallics.
watch_item: Ion irradiation occurs at much higher dose rates than reactor neutron conditions, so the balance of mechanisms may differ in-service.
paper_title: CuCrZr heat-sink irradiation performance reveals new challenges for thermonuclear fusion reactors
